title: What I think about the 1st Ammendment

transcript.text: Hello. This video is what I think about the First Amendment. I think it is what makes America. What is America? What sets America apart from any other country? Three countries. It's been like probably nine years out of the country and I think America is a place that people really do value a little bit, ten minutes or less.
We had a little web page about it. So let's review what it is.

Congress shall make no law respecting an establishment of religion or prohibiting the free exercise thereof or abridging the freedom of speech or of the press or the right of the people peaceably to assemble hence to petition the government for a redress of greed.

You have, I guess, two clauses. Two parts to that. You have the establishment clause and the free exercise clause. So the establishment clause, let's say, is that we're not going to be a country ruled by one religion.
Like, say, England was, even after the break from the Catholic Church, where if you want to think something or say something, it has to be okay with the deacon or whatever that guy is called.
The Catholic Church. If you want to, you know, say the world's round, you don't have to get permission from the Pope or else you get excommunicated, sent to hell or whatever or into the state.
That's pretty much common knowledge. I think the main thing about the first amendment is going to be the free exercise, where you can say whatever you want.
The right of the people peaceably to assemble in the petition, the government for a redress of grievances or abridging them.
Now, I've been looking at all of the Supreme Court cases that have come up that have gone, I guess, to the U.S. Supreme Court and then various state Supreme Courts.
And, um, a lot of, most of these, uh, these cases, if you were just to look at them, especially the ones that happened before, like, the 60s, is in the right.
Um, you get these, you get these, uh, cases where, say, in the 60s, one was where, uh, kids couldn't wear, kids wore armbands, black armbands, to protest the Vietnam War.
And they all got, like, uh, kicked out of school or something like that. I don't know exactly what it was.
Um, now, that's obviously wrong. Um, and there have been more. Um, like, a kid made a website about slandering a teacher that they didn't like.
So they were, um, kicked out of school for that. And I've been looking at these cases, and most of them have to do with that.
People, um, now, I don't know anything about law, I don't know what slander is, but people going insulting their teachers and then getting kicked out of school.
Now, most of the time, I don't have the time to go through this, I'm already almost halfway through, but most of the time, the Supreme Court sides with the person.
Um, now, I agree with that. Um, that it just seems silly that you would spend and waste all of this taxpayer's money going to court.
Well, I guess if you have to solidify what it actually is, but going to court and trying to just be vindictive against people just for expressing themselves.
If I was a teacher or somebody in power and somebody, some student of mine made a website slandering me, I wouldn't try to be vindictive and get revenge on them.
Um, I don't think we need laws and threaten people and make them not want to be afraid to, um, insult you because of repercussion.
It's kind of, it's barbaric to me. Um, I think if people could just freely do that, then, you know, people would do it.
And then, it would stop. Um, if you let people insult you, yeah, you'll be insulted, but eventually, people will find other things to do.
And you don't have to. Um, let me see if there's some other, other kinds of Supreme Court cases.
There's another kind of Supreme Court case of people respecting the Establishment Clause.
And that seems to be, like, a lot of it's just money-related. People avoiding taxes and stuff like this.
And that's, uh, that's kind of, I think, abuse of it.
There's one where a woman, well, one example of that would be how, uh, I guess some Jehovah's Witnesses went from door to door.
And they had a record that's called the Catholic Enemies, and they wanted to sue them or something like that, you know.
And that's just silly. That's like starting some kind of religious war.
You should let people say whatever you want.
If you get all huffy and puffy because somebody slanders you, then you just, you don't have enough confidence in yourself in the first place.
And you don't need to be wasting taxpayers' money trying to go after people for that.
Now, this other thing that I came across, I've got these statistics on this page I made, just of what people think about how free we are, how free we think we are.
And it appears to me that Americans, for the most part, we believe that we're as free as we need to be.
For example, um, let's see, should musicians be allowed to sing songs that others might find offensive?
And only 23% strongly agreed in 97.
And, uh, and it did, and it has been steadily creeping up, you know, from 23 to 27 in 99, and 32 in 2000, 34 in 2001, 35 in 2003, and then up to 41 in 2006.
But, basically, what that tells me is that, um, you're only as free as the citizens, you're only as free, the citizens of the country are only as free as they allow themselves to.

The whole reason, and, uh, the whole reason we have all these Supreme Court cases of kids trying to defend themselves and not being kicked out of school, I mean, that's their education, let them stay in school.
If you want to teach them to not disrespect their elders and let them teach them, don't kick them out of school and not let them come to learn.
Um, but, uh, anyways, it's these people who are oppressing in the small term, in the small scale.
And, uh, it ends up being the, uh, our national law that ends up freeing the students.
So, um, basically, you're only as free.
Kind of like, uh, you know, the French Revolution, where you weren't ready to have a democracy.
The people weren't ready to have a democracy until they all collectively decided that they were going to.
Multinational corporations are actually good.
They're just funded by people who invest in them.
They don't even know that they're investing in them.
Anyways, I'm way far left in terms of, of, uh, social freedom of speech.
People, if you say whatever the heck they want, then, uh, we're eventually going to rise to the top as far as the society.
If you try to stifle people in any way, it'll just make them angry and they'll protest more.
And then they'll have to fight for it and that will just cost money.
So, that's what I think about the First Amendment.
Out of time.
I'll make a longer video later.
Bye-bye.
